audioscript.text: Part 1, you will hear a woman called Jan, phoning a man about their local conservation group.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 1 to 5.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 1 to 5.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hello.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: Oh hello, my name is Jan.
audioscript.speaker: JAN

audioscript.text: Are you the right person to talk to about the Buckworth Conservation Group?
audioscript.speaker: JAN

audioscript.text: Yes, I'm Peter, I'm the secretary.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: Good, I've just moved to this area, and I'm interested in getting involved.
audioscript.speaker: JAN

audioscript.text: I was in a similar group where I used to live.
audioscript.speaker: JAN

audioscript.text: Could you tell me something about your activities, please?
audioscript.speaker: JAN

audioscript.text: Of course.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: Well we have a mixture of regular activities and special events.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: One of the regular ones is trying to keep the beach free of litter.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: A few of us spend a couple of hours a month on it.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: And it's awful how much there is to clear.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: I wish people would be more responsible and take it home with them.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: I totally agree.
audioscript.speaker: JAN

audioscript.text: I'd be happy to help with that.
audioscript.speaker: JAN

audioscript.text: Is it OK to take dogs?
audioscript.speaker: JAN

audioscript.text: I'm afraid not, as they're banned from the beach itself.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: You can take them along the cliffs though, and children are welcome.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: JAN

audioscript.text: We also manage a nature reserve, and there's a lot to do there all year round.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: Uh. For example, because it's a popular place to visit, we spend a lot of time looking after the paths.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: And making sure they're in good condition for walking.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: I could certainly help with that.
audioscript.speaker: JAN

audioscript.text: Good.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: And we have a program of creating new habitats there.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: We've just finished making and installing nesting boxes for birds to use, and next we're going to work on encouraging insects.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: They're important for the biodiversity of the reserve.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: They certainly are.
audioscript.speaker: JAN

audioscript.text: Oh, and we're also running a project to identify the different species of butterflies that visit the reserve.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: You might be interested in taking part in that.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: Sure, I was involved in something similar where I used to live, counting all the species of moths.
audioscript.speaker: JAN

audioscript.text: I'd enjoy that.
audioscript.speaker: JAN

audioscript.text: Another job we're doing at the reserve is replacing the wall on the southern side.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: Between the parking area and our woodshed, it was badly damaged in a storm last month.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: OK.
audioscript.speaker: JAN

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the conversation, you have some time to look at questions 6 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 6 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Then, as I said, we have a program of events as well, both at the weekend and during the week.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: JAN

audioscript.text: I presume you have guided walks.
audioscript.speaker: JAN

audioscript.text: I'd like to get to know the local countryside, as I'm new to the area.
audioscript.speaker: JAN

audioscript.text: Yes, we do.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: The next walk is to Ruston Island a week on Saturday.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: We'll be meeting in the car park at Dunsmore Beach at low tide.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: That's when the sands are dry enough for us to walk to the island without getting wet.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: Sounds good.
audioscript.speaker: JAN

audioscript.text: The island's a great place to explore. It's quite small, and it's got a range of habitats.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: It's also an ideal location for seeing seals just off the coast or even on the beach.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: OK. And is there anything we should bring?
audioscript.speaker: JAN

audioscript.text: Like a picnic for instance.
audioscript.speaker: JAN

audioscript.text: Yes, do bring one, as it's a full-day walk, and of course it'll be wet walking across and back, so make sure your boots are waterproof.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: I must buy a new pair.
audioscript.speaker: JAN

audioscript.text: There's a hole in one of my current ones.
audioscript.speaker: JAN

audioscript.text: Well, I'd definitely like to come on the walk.
audioscript.speaker: JAN

audioscript.text: Great.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: Then later this month, we're having a one-day woodwork session in Hopton Wood.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: I've never tried that before.
audioscript.speaker: JAN

audioscript.text: Is it OK for beginners to take part?
audioscript.speaker: JAN

audioscript.text: Definitely, there'll be a couple of experts leading the session, and we keep the number of participants down, so you'll get as much help as you need.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: Excellent! I'd love to be able to make chairs.
audioscript.speaker: JAN

audioscript.text: Ha. That's probably too ambitious for one day!
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: You'll be starting with wooden spoons, and of course learning how to use the tools, and anything you make is yours to take home with you.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: That sounds like fun.
audioscript.speaker: JAN

audioscript.text: When is it?
audioscript.speaker: JAN

audioscript.text: It's on the 17th, from 10 a.m. until 3.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: There's a charge of £35 including lunch,
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: or £40, if you want to camp in the wood.
audioscript.speaker: PETER

audioscript.text: I should think I'll come home the same day.
audioscript.speaker: JAN

audioscript.text: Well, I'd certainly like to join the group.
audioscript.speaker: JAN

audioscript.text: It sounds like you've...
audioscript.speaker: JAN

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers to part 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Part 2, you will hear a tour guide, Lou Miller, speaking to a group of people about a boat trip they are going to take around the Australian island of Tasmania.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 14.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 11 to 14.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: So, hello everyone, my name is Lou Miller, and I'm going to be your tour guide today, as we take this fantastic boat trip around the Tasmanian coast.
audioscript.speaker: LOU MILLER

audioscript.text: Before we set off, I just want to tell you a few things about our journey.
audioscript.speaker: LOU MILLER

audioscript.text: Our boats aren't huge, as you can see, we already have three staff members on board.
audioscript.speaker: LOU MILLER

audioscript.text: And on top of that, we can transport a further 15 people, that's you, around the coastline.
audioscript.speaker: LOU MILLER

audioscript.text: But please note, if there are more than 9 people on either side of the boat, we'll move some of you over.
audioscript.speaker: LOU MILLER

audioscript.text: Ha, otherwise all 18 of us will end up in the sea.
audioscript.speaker: LOU MILLER

audioscript.text: We've recently upgraded all our boats.
audioscript.speaker: LOU MILLER

audioscript.text: They used to be jet black, but our new ones now have these comfortable dark red seats and a light green exterior.
audioscript.speaker: LOU MILLER

audioscript.text: In order to stand out from others and help promote our company.
audioscript.speaker: LOU MILLER

audioscript.text: This gives our boats a rather unique appearance, don't you think?
audioscript.speaker: LOU MILLER

audioscript.text: We offer you a free lunch box during the trip.
audioscript.speaker: LOU MILLER

audioscript.text: And we have three types.
audioscript.speaker: LOU MILLER

audioscript.text: Lunch Box 1 contains ham and tomato sandwiches.
audioscript.speaker: LOU MILLER

audioscript.text: Lunch Box 2 contains a cheddar cheese roll.
audioscript.speaker: LOU MILLER

audioscript.text: And lunch box 3 is salad based, and also contains eggs and tuna.
audioscript.speaker: LOU MILLER

audioscript.text: All three lunch boxes also have a packet of crisps and chocolate bar inside.
audioscript.speaker: LOU MILLER

audioscript.text: Please let staff know which lunch box you prefer.
audioscript.speaker: LOU MILLER

audioscript.text: I'm sure I don't have to ask you not to throw anything into the sea.
audioscript.speaker: LOU MILLER

audioscript.text: We don't have any bins to put litter in, but Jess, myself or Ray, our other guide, will collect it from you after lunch.
audioscript.speaker: LOU MILLER

audioscript.text: And put it all in a large plastic sack.
audioscript.speaker: LOU MILLER

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 15 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 15 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: The engine on the boat makes quite a lot of noise, so before we head off, let me tell you a few things about what you're going to see.
audioscript.speaker: LOU MILLER

audioscript.text: This area is famous for its ancient lighthouse, which you'll see from the boat as we turn past the first little island.
audioscript.speaker: LOU MILLER

audioscript.text: It was built in 1838 to protect sailors, as a number of shipwrecks had led to significant loss of life.
audioscript.speaker: LOU MILLER

audioscript.text: The construction itself was complicated, as some of the original drawings kept by the local council show.
audioscript.speaker: LOU MILLER

audioscript.text: It sits right on top of the cliffs in a very isolated spot.
audioscript.speaker: LOU MILLER

audioscript.text: In the 19th century, there were many jobs there, such as polishing the brass lamps, chopping firewood, and cleaning windows that kept lighthouse keepers busy.
audioscript.speaker: LOU MILLER

audioscript.text: These workers were mainly prison convicts until the middle of that century, when ordinary families willing to live in such circumstances took over.
audioscript.speaker: LOU MILLER

audioscript.text: Some of you have asked me what creatures we can expect to see.
audioscript.speaker: LOU MILLER

audioscript.text: I know everyone loves the penguins, but they're very shy, and unfortunately tend to hide from passing boats.
audioscript.speaker: LOU MILLER

audioscript.text: But you might see birds in the distance, such as sea eagles flying around the cliff edges where they nest.
audioscript.speaker: LOU MILLER

audioscript.text: When we get to the rocky area inhabited by fur seals, we'll stop and watch them swimming around the coast.
audioscript.speaker: LOU MILLER

audioscript.text: They're inquisitive creatures, so don't be surprised if one pops up right in front of you.
audioscript.speaker: LOU MILLER

audioscript.text: Their predators, orca whales, hunt along the coastline too.
audioscript.speaker: LOU MILLER

audioscript.text: But spotting one of these is rare.
audioscript.speaker: LOU MILLER

audioscript.text: Dolphins on the other hand can sometimes approach on their own or in groups, as they ride the waves beside us.
audioscript.speaker: LOU MILLER

audioscript.text: Lastly, I want to mention the caves.
audioscript.speaker: LOU MILLER

audioscript.text: Tasmania is famous for its caves, and the ones we'll pass by are so amazing that people are lost for words when they see them.
audioscript.speaker: LOU MILLER

audioscript.text: They can only be approached by sea, but if you feel that you want to see more than we're able to show you, then you can take a kayak into the area on another day.
audioscript.speaker: LOU MILLER

audioscript.text: And one of our staff will give you more information on that.
audioscript.speaker: LOU MILLER

audioscript.text: What we'll do is to go through a narrow channel, past some incredible rock formations.
audioscript.speaker: LOU MILLER

audioscript.text: And from there, we'll be able to see the openings to the caves, and at that point, we'll talk to you about what lies beyond.
audioscript.speaker: LOU MILLER

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers to part 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: When we get to the rocky area inhabited by fur seals, we'll stop and watch them swimming around the coast.
utterances.speaker: LOU MILLER

utterances.text: They're inquisitive creatures, so don't be surprised if one pops up right in front of you.
utterances.speaker: LOU MILLER

utterances.text: Their predators, orca whales, hunt along the coastline too.
utterances.speaker: LOU MILLER

utterances.text: But spotting one of these is rare.
utterances.speaker: LOU MILLER

utterances.text: Dolphins on the other hand can sometimes approach on their own or in groups, as they ride the waves beside us.
utterances.speaker: LOU MILLER

utterances.text: Lastly, I want to mention the caves.
utterances.speaker: LOU MILLER

utterances.text: Tasmania is famous for its caves, and the ones we'll pass by are so amazing that people are lost for words when they see them.
utterances.speaker: LOU MILLER

utterances.text: They can only be approached by sea, but if you feel that you want to see more than we're able to show you, then you can take a kayak into the area on another day.
utterances.speaker: LOU MILLER

utterances.text: And one of our staff will give you more information on that.
utterances.speaker: LOU MILLER

utterances.text: What we'll do is to go through a narrow channel, past some incredible rock formations.
utterances.speaker: LOU MILLER

utterances.text: And from there, we'll be able to see the openings to the caves, and at that point, we'll talk to you about what lies beyond.
utterances.speaker: LOU MILLER

utterances.text: That is the end of part 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers to part 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: What is the maximum number of people who can stand on each side of the boat?
What colour are the tour boats?
options: dark red
jet black
light green

items.title: Which lunchbox is suitable for someone who doesn't eat meat or fish?
options: Lunchbox 1
Lunchbox 2
Lunchbox 3

items.title: What should people do with their litter?
options: take it home
hand it to a member of staff
put it in the bins provided on the boat

body.title: Which TWO features of the lighthouse does Lou mention?
options: why it was built
who built it
how long it took to build
who staffed it
what it was built with

body.title: Which TWO types of creature might come close to the boat?
options: sea eagles
fur seals
dolphins
whales
penguins

body.title: Which TWO points does Lou make about the caves?
options: Only large tourist boats can visit them.
The entrances to them are often blocked.
It is too dangerous for individuals to go near them.
Someone will explain what is inside them.
They cannot be reached on foot.

test_text.title: Work experience for veterinary science students

audioscript.text: Part 3, you will hear two veterinary science students called Diana and Tim discussing their work placements and their course modules.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 21 to 26.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 21 to 26.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: So, Tim, we have to do a short summary of our work experience on a farm.
audioscript.speaker: DIANA

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: My farm was great, but arranging the work experience was hard.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: One problem was it was miles away, and I don't drive.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: And also, I'd really wanted a placement for a month, but I could only get one for two weeks.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: Hmm. I was lucky.
audioscript.speaker: DIANA

audioscript.text: The farmer let me stay on the farm, so I didn't have to travel.
audioscript.speaker: DIANA

audioscript.text: But finding the right sort of farm to apply to wasn't easy.
audioscript.speaker: DIANA

audioscript.text: No, they don't seem to have websites, do they?
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: I found mine through a friend of my mother's, but it wasn't easy.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: No.
audioscript.speaker: DIANA

audioscript.text: My farm was mostly livestock, especially sheep.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: I really enjoyed helping out with them.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: I was up most of one night helping a sheep deliver a lamb.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: On your own?
audioscript.speaker: DIANA

audioscript.text: No, the farmer was there, and he told me what to do.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: It wasn't a straightforward birth.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: But I managed.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: It was a great feeling to see the lamb stagger to its feet and start feeding almost straight away, and to know that it was OK.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: Hmm. Then another time a lamb had broken its leg, and they got the vet in to set it, and he talked me through what he was doing.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: That was really useful.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: Yes, my farm had sheep too.
audioscript.speaker: DIANA

audioscript.text: The farm was in a valley, and they had a lowland breed called Suffolks, although the farmer said they'd had other breeds in the past.
audioscript.speaker: DIANA

audioscript.text: So were they bred for their meat?
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: Mostly, yes.
audioscript.speaker: DIANA

audioscript.text: They're quite big and solid.
audioscript.speaker: DIANA

audioscript.text: My farm was up in the hills, and they had a different breed of sheep.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: They were Cheviots.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: Oh.
audioscript.speaker: DIANA

audioscript.text: I heard their wool's really sought after.
audioscript.speaker: DIANA

audioscript.text: Yes, it's very hard wearing, and they use it for carpets.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: DIANA

audioscript.text: I was interested in the amount of supplements they add to animals' feed nowadays.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: Like, even the chickens got extra vitamins and electrolytes in their feed.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: Yes, I found that too, and they're not cheap.
audioscript.speaker: DIANA

audioscript.text: But my farmer said some are overpriced for what they are.
audioscript.speaker: DIANA

audioscript.text: And he didn't give them as a matter of routine, just at times when the chickens seemed to particularly require them.
audioscript.speaker: DIANA

audioscript.text: Yes, mine said the same.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: He said certain breeds of chickens might need more supplements than the others.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: But the cheap and expensive ones are all basically the same.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: So did your farm have any other livestock, Diana?
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: Yes, dairy cows.
audioscript.speaker: DIANA

audioscript.text: Uh. I made a really embarrassing mistake when I was working in the milk shed.
audioscript.speaker: DIANA

audioscript.text: Some cows had been treated with antibiotics, so their milk wasn't suitable for human consumption.
audioscript.speaker: DIANA

audioscript.text: And it had to be put in a separate container, but I got mixed up, and I poured some milk from the wrong cow in with the milk for humans, so the whole lot had to be thrown away.
audioscript.speaker: DIANA

audioscript.text: The farmer wasn't too happy with me.
audioscript.speaker: DIANA

audioscript.text: I asked my farmer how much he depended on the vet to deal with health problems.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: I'd read reports that the livestock's health is being affected.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: As farmers are under pressure to increase production.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: Well, he didn't agree with that, but he said that actually some of the stuff the vets do like minor operations.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: He'd be quite capable of doing himself.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: Yeah. My farmer said the same. But he reckons vets' skills are still needed.
audioscript.speaker: DIANA

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the conversation, you have some time to look at questions 27 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 27 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now we've got to give a bit of feedback about last term's modules, just short comments, apparently.
audioscript.speaker: DIANA

audioscript.text: Shall we do that now?
audioscript.speaker: DIANA

audioscript.text: OK, so medical terminology.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: Well, my heart sank when I saw that, especially right at the beginning of the course. And I did struggle with it.
audioscript.speaker: DIANA

audioscript.text: I thought it would be hard, but actually I found it all quite straightforward.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: What did you think about diet and nutrition?
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: OK, I suppose.
audioscript.speaker: DIANA

audioscript.text: Do you remember what they told us about pet food, and the fact that there's such limited checking into whether or not it's contaminated?
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: I mean in comparison with the checks on food for humans, I thought that was terrible.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: Hmm.
audioscript.speaker: DIANA

audioscript.text: I think the module that really impressed me was the animal disease one.
audioscript.speaker: DIANA

audioscript.text: When we looked at domesticated animals in different parts of the world, like camels and water buffalo and alpaca, the economies of so many countries depend on these.
audioscript.speaker: DIANA

audioscript.text: But scientists don't know much about the diseases that affect them.
audioscript.speaker: DIANA

audioscript.text: Yes, I thought they'd know a lot about ways of controlling and eradicating those diseases, but that's not the case at all.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: I loved the wildlife medication unit.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: Things like helping birds that have been caught in oil spills.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: That's something I hadn't thought about before.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: Yeah.
audioscript.speaker: DIANA

audioscript.text: I thought I might write my dissertation on something connected with that.
audioscript.speaker: DIANA

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: So...
audioscript.speaker: TIM

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers to part 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: What problem did both Diana and Tim have when arranging their work experience?
options: making initial contact with suitable farms
organising transport to and from the farm
finding a placement for the required length of time

items.title: Tim was pleased to be able to help
options: a lamb that had a broken leg.
a sheep that was having difficulty giving birth.
a newly born lamb that was having trouble feeding.

items.title: Diana says the sheep on her farm
options: were of various different varieties
were mainly reared for their meat
had better quality wool than sheep on the hills

items.title: What did the students learn about adding supplements to chicken feed?
options: These should only be given if specially needed.
It is worth paying extra for the most effective ones.
The amount given at one time should be limited.

items.title: What happened when Diana was working with dairy cows?
options: She identified some cows incorrectly.
She accidentally threw some milk away.
She made a mistake when storing milk.

items.title: What did both farmers mention about vets and farming?
options: Vets are failing to cope with some aspects of animal health.
There needs to be a fundamental change in the training of vets.
Some jobs could be done by the farmer rather than by a vet.

body.title: Work experience for veterinary science students

text: What opinion do the students give about each of the following modules on their veterinary science course?
textReadable: What opinion do the students give about each of the following modules on their veterinary science course?

list: Tim found this easier than expected.
Tim thought this was not very clearly organised.
Diana may do some further study on this.
They both found the reading required for this was difficult.
Tim was shocked at something he learned on this module.
They were both surprised how little is known about some aspects of this.
items: Medical terminology <input>
Diet and nutrition  <input>
Animal disease      <input>
Wildlife medication <input>
body.title: Modules on Veterinary Science course

audioscript.text: Part 4, you will hear an anthropology student giving a presentation on spiral path designs known as labyrinths.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Labyrinths have existed for well over 4,000 years.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Labyrinths and labyrinthine symbols have been found in regions as diverse as modern day Turkey.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Ireland, Greece and India.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There are various designs of labyrinth, but what they all have in common is a winding spiral path, which leads to a central area.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There is one starting point at the entrance, and the goal is to reach the central area.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Finding your way through a labyrinth involves many twists and turns.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But it's not possible to get lost, as there is only one single path.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In modern times, the word labyrinth has taken on a different meaning, and is often used as a synonym for a maze.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: A maze is quite different.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As it is a kind of puzzle with an intricate network of paths.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Mazes became fashionable in the 15th and 16th centuries in Europe, and can still be found in the gardens of great houses and palaces.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The paths are usually surrounded by thick high hedges, so that it's not possible to see over them.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Entering a maze usually involves getting lost a few times.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Before using logic to work out the pattern and find your way to the center, and then out again.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There are lots of dead ends and paths which lead you back to where you started.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The word maze is believed to come from a Scandinavian word for a state of confusion.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This is where the word amazing comes from.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Labyrinths on the other hand have a very different function, although people now often refer to things they find complicated as labyrinths.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This is not how they were seen in the past.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The winding spiral of the labyrinth has been used for centuries as a metaphor for life's journey.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It served as a spiritual reminder that there is purpose and meaning to our lives.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And helped to give people a sense of direction.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Labyrinths are thought to encourage a feeling of calm, and have been used as a meditation and prayer tool in many cultures over many centuries.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The earliest examples of the labyrinth spiral pattern have been found carved into stone from Sardinia to Scandinavia, from Arizona to India.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: To Africa.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In Europe, these spiral carvings date from the late Bronze Age.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The Native American Pima tribe wove baskets with a circular labyrinth design.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That depicted their own cosmology, in ancient Greece, the labyrinth spiral was used on coins around 4,000 years ago.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Labyrinths made of mosaics were commonly found in bathhouses, villas, and tombs throughout the Roman Empire.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In Northern Europe, there were actual physical labyrinths designed for walking on.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: These were cut into the turf or grass, usually in a circular pattern.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The origin of these walking labyrinths remains unclear, but they were probably used for fertility rites, which may date back thousands of years.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: 11 examples of turf labyrinths survive today.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Including the largest one at Saffron Walden in England, which used to have a large tree in the middle of it.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: More recently, labyrinths have experienced something of a revival.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Some believe that walking a labyrinth promotes healing and mindfulness, and there are those who believe in its emotional and physical benefits,
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: which include slower breathing and a restored sense of balance and perspective.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This idea has become so popular that labyrinths have been laid into the floors of spas, wellness centers, and even prisons in recent years.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: A pamphlet at Colorado Children's Hospital informs patients that 'walking a labyrinth can often calm people in the midst of a crisis'.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And apparently, it's not only patients who benefit.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Many visitors find walking a labyrinth less stressful than sitting in a corridor or waiting room.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Some doctors even walk the labyrinth during their breaks.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In some hospitals, patients who can't walk can have a paper finger labyrinth brought to their bed.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The science behind the theory is a little sketchy, but there are dozens of small-scale studies which support claims about the benefits of labyrinths.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: For example, one study found that walking a labyrinth provided 'short-term calming, relaxation and relief from anxiety' for Alzheimer's patients.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So what is it about labyrinths that makes their appeal so universal?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have one minute to check your answers to part 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Part 4, you will hear an anthropology student giving a presentation on spiral path designs known as labyrinths.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Labyrinths have existed for well over 4,000 years.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Labyrinths and labyrinthine symbols have been found in regions as diverse as modern day Turkey.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Ireland, Greece and India.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: There are various designs of labyrinth, but what they all have in common is a winding spiral path, which leads to a central area.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: There is one starting point at the entrance, and the goal is to reach the central area.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Finding your way through a labyrinth involves many twists and turns.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But it's not possible to get lost, as there is only one single path.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In modern times, the word labyrinth has taken on a different meaning, and is often used as a synonym for a maze.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: A maze is quite different.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: As it is a kind of puzzle with an intricate network of paths.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Mazes became fashionable in the 15th and 16th centuries in Europe, and can still be found in the gardens of great houses and palaces.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The paths are usually surrounded by thick high hedges, so that it's not possible to see over them.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Entering a maze usually involves getting lost a few times.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Before using logic to work out the pattern and find your way to the center, and then out again.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: There are lots of dead ends and paths which lead you back to where you started.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The word maze is believed to come from a Scandinavian word for a state of confusion.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This is where the word amazing comes from.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Labyrinths on the other hand have a very different function, although people now often refer to things they find complicated as labyrinths.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This is not how they were seen in the past.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The winding spiral of the labyrinth has been used for centuries as a metaphor for life's journey.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It served as a spiritual reminder that there is purpose and meaning to our lives.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And helped to give people a sense of direction.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Labyrinths are thought to encourage a feeling of calm, and have been used as a meditation and prayer tool in many cultures over many centuries.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The earliest examples of the labyrinth spiral pattern have been found carved into stone from Sardinia to Scandinavia, from Arizona to India.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: To Africa.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In Europe, these spiral carvings date from the late Bronze Age.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The Native American Pima tribe wove baskets with a circular labyrinth design.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That depicted their own cosmology, in ancient Greece, the labyrinth spiral was used on coins around 4,000 years ago.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Labyrinths made of mosaics were commonly found in bathhouses, villas, and tombs throughout the Roman Empire.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In Northern Europe, there were actual physical labyrinths designed for walking on.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: These were cut into the turf or grass, usually in a circular pattern.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The origin of these walking labyrinths remains unclear, but they were probably used for fertility rites, which may date back thousands of years.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: 11 examples of turf labyrinths survive today.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Including the largest one at Saffron Walden in England, which used to have a large tree in the middle of it.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: More recently, labyrinths have experienced something of a revival.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Some believe that walking a labyrinth promotes healing and mindfulness, and there are those who believe in its emotional and physical benefits,
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: which include slower breathing and a restored sense of balance and perspective.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This idea has become so popular that labyrinths have been laid into the floors of spas, wellness centers, and even prisons in recent years.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: A pamphlet at Colorado Children's Hospital informs patients that 'walking a labyrinth can often calm people in the midst of a crisis'.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And apparently, it's not only patients who benefit.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Many visitors find walking a labyrinth less stressful than sitting in a corridor or waiting room.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Some doctors even walk the labyrinth during their breaks.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In some hospitals, patients who can't walk can have a paper finger labyrinth brought to their bed.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The science behind the theory is a little sketchy, but there are dozens of small-scale studies which support claims about the benefits of labyrinths.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: For example, one study found that walking a labyrinth provided 'short-term calming, relaxation and relief from anxiety' for Alzheimer's patients.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So what is it about labyrinths that makes their appeal so universal?
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is the end of part 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have one minute to check your answers to part 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items: a winding spiral path leading to a central area
